summary: Spolsky shares anecdotal experience comparing pair programming with traditional solo development plus code review at Fog Creek. After trying pair programming on a client project, the team found they moved faster working individually on CityDesk. He acknowledges the irony of using anecdotes as data, but argues that lightweight code review using CVS/FogBUGZ integration provides the quality benefits of pair programming without the productivity cost.
key_points: Pair programming provided 'continual code review' benefits but at significant productivity cost compared to solo work
Solo developers with tooling-assisted code review moved faster, 'blasting through dozens of fixes per person' per day
Lightweight code review (10 minutes total) caught problems while being far cheaper than full-time pairing
Good tooling (CVS/FogBUGZ integration with two-click graphical diffs) makes code review nearly frictionless
money_quotes: I have to say that we're moving even faster without pairing up, literally blasting through dozens of fixes and small features a day per person.
Q: To a Political Scientist, what is the singular of the word "data"? A: "Anecdote"
I've gathered some data which shows that the benefits of pair programming are not enough to offset the loss in productivity.
Today I spent about 10 minutes total doing code reviews (found some problems, even) which is really cheap compared to pair programming.
The 'continual code review' made things go very quickly.
key_insight: Lightweight, tool-assisted code review captures most of pair programming's quality benefits at a fraction of the productivity cost.
